speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Goszer, you were telling us about the time when you moved from your home into a street designated for the Jews. Can you tell us more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So when everybody came out from their hidings-- that escaped to the hills or to the forest-- they came back. And everybody moved in on one street. So there were two, three, four survivors from families. And they were living together. And life had to go on-- go back to work. And get the 250 crumbs of bread a day. And people got used to it. I mean, it was such a thing that it's hard to explain. Then, when you lose a father, a mother, a brother, a sister in one day, and next day you come, and life has to go on. We weren't used to such things. But that's what we did. There was a big organized Judenrat. You know what a Judenrat is. And so, often-- every week, or every second day or third day-- the police came with a letter that they want 100,000 marks. And if the Judenrat would not give it to them, they will start again action. And they gave you time-- let's say, two hours. And in two hours, must organize to-- 100,000 marks. But it's survivors, and given to them. Then I recall, I was at home that day. And it was in spring. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was somewhere in a village, a German killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the second day, came a group of Germans. And they were called a [GERMAN] group, this-- a group that destroyed people. And they were catching people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was caught at that time, too. And they brought us to a place that there was an old castle. And they put us out on the walls, straight. There was probably about 28 or 29 young people. And I was included. I was the last one at the corner standing. And in front of us were three Germans with machine guns, pointing at that wall. And the officer came down to us, looked at his watch, and said it's a quarter to 1:00. If you don't tell us till a quarter till 2:00, who has [GERMAN], that means contact with the partisans-- if you don't tell us, we'll shoot you all. He need to have a cigarette, you know, smoked and walked around. And I was standing so, in front on the three machine guns, maybe 15, 20 meters away from us. And as I was standing at the end, I was moving slowly-- 1, 2, 3, till I came completely to the edge. And I took off. And there was a river down. And I escaped. And I didn't hear any shooting, anything. And I can never understand the way they looked at me with the machine guns. And when I was escaping, they didn't shoot. I don't know. And I escaped. Later on, nobody from the fellows said, who has contact with partisans. And they shot them all, right there. And they brought out another few Jews from the street where we used to live. And they buried them right there. I came home-- my mother was there, my sister was there. And we talked about that, a day two. And you forget about it. Life has to go on, because you're living. Went back to work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And working a week or two or three, rumors came that the Gestapo came to the city. And they will kill again Jews. So the relatives of the parents of the people what they were working in the sugar factory said, don't come home. Sleep at the sugar factory because there is so much, there's going to be, again, killing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in the sugar factory after work. We went to the stables, because the sugar factory had horses and cows. And-- and we slept there.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 9:30 or 10 o'clock, the sugar factory was surrounded by the Ukrainian police and by the Gestapo. And they went everywhere in the sugar factory. And they find every Jew. And they put us out in fours. And they-- we marched to the city, because the sugar factory was about 3 kilometers, 2 kilometers from the city. And they brought us to the police station. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they locked us up in a room. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] About 11:30, or quarter to 12:00, I said to a friend of mine-- and the police station was on the second floor, and we were locked up on the second floor. It was a Jewish home. But the police took it away-- took it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to the fellow, why don't we escape? We haven't got what to lose. I say, we'll knock on the door, a policeman probably will come and ask us what we want. And that's the way it was. So we tell him that we want to go to the washroom. And we didn't have washrooms like here-- water inside. But we had outhouses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was, from the room where we were locked up to go to the-- through the corridor, probably about 5 meters down. We had to turn right. But as we turned right, there was a fence. And the washrooms were downstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as we turned right, I jumped the fence. And my friend jumped the fence. And he shot once, and that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I escaped to the right and my friend to the left. And I came home. And at that time, there was a change in the shift in the sugar factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember the siren-- I heard the siren. So I knew it's exactly 12 o'clock. So I came home, and I told this to my mother and sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I have to go back in the hiding, in case they will follow me. But in the morning, I said, you go out on the street and cry that I was caught in that thing, just to mislead them. In the morning, she went and cried, and they caught her son. And she was beside the police in their trucks. German trucks came, packed all the people and drove them away on the north side. They took them away. And we never heard from them. Or they went to work, or they were killed. We never knew anything about it. But in 1944, when my hometown was liberated, Ukrainian people from the village came and told us that there is a group of people that the Ukrainian police shot them. So in no time at all, the Russians, in 1944-- because my hometown was liberated-- the Russians put up a monument there, the Russian citizens who were killed in that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, did you have any contact with some of your Ukrainian friends from before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the majority of the young police fellows-- but they were in the police. They were friends of mine before the war. For an example, I never wore a yellow [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mark on my clothes. They used to come to the house. I remember one occasion, two fellows came-- two police fellows came. I was in the bedroom, lying. And my mother was in the living room. And they were talking to her, and I kept quiet. I didn't know if they were going to take me or they're going to shoot me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I was lying on a bed. And I had-- and I had a loaded pistol. And I thought to myself, if they're going to kill me or take me away, I will defend myself. But no. They came into the bedroom, they saw me. And they talked to me. And they didn't touch me at all. A matter of fact, I went through the occupation that nobody touched me in the wartime. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then in 1942, came Sukkot. And that was the liquidation of the Jews in my hometown. Ukrainian police came from all over, Gestapo, and they surrounded again the city. And they-- anybody they caught, they took him to the forest. And they shot him. There were pits made out. And that was Judenfrei-- anyone could discover a Jew. The Ukrainian police offered a bag of sugar, because sugar-- or salt even-- they used to offer. So weeks after that, they used to still catch Jewish people in the forest at farmers'. There were farmers that they used to accept Jews to hide them. But they took away everything from them. They kept them a week or two. And they-- or they killed them, themselves, on their property and buried them. Or they brought them to the city to the police. Mine family, when it came Sukkot-- Judenfrei-- we were still in that cave. But there was no use to sit there anymore. There wasn't enough air-- after three days, that wasn't enough air. We had dry bread. We prepared, in case we have to sit a few days. But there was no use anymore to sit. So we discussed about it, talked about it till we  say, we're leaving. And that was early in the morning. It was dark. So I remember. I went out first. And I went out to take a look on the street. The only thing I was able to see at that time, a lot of throwing their chair on the street-- a lot of the chairs that the-- Ukrainians used to come and take everything away from their houses. So I came back to the house, and I said, let's go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mom, and my sister, my brother, and I, we decided to go to a farmer that my father used to do business with him. So we escaped. We knew our way out to get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we crossed the river-- there was a river called Kalchik-- to go to the farms, it was already light, starting to bright up the day. And the police at that time were driving on bicycles, on horses. Just anywhere they saw a Jew, take everything away from him and kill him. And as we walked, there was a Ukrainian police was approaching us, in front on us. And there was no use to escape, left or right, because he would have shot us anyways. So we've thought, maybe if we approach him and we'll talk to him, maybe he'll let us go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the policeman had a rifle with him. And he was on a horse. And he came down.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wore a fur jacket that I did not give to Germans. And I wore long boots. And the policeman got up from the horse, and he liked my boots, and he liked my fur jacket. And he told me I should take it off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took off the jacket, and I threw it on the ground. So he tells me to take off the boots. But the boots-- I had to put my foot and take it off because it wasn't just like a shoe to put on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I pretended it's hard to take it off-- hard to take it off. So he came to pull it off. So I jumped on the policeman. And actually-- all of us, even my mother, jumped on him. And we wrestled him down to the floor. And at that time, it was we survive, or he survives-- one of us. And we left him lying on the ground. And we went farther down. And we got into the bush. And we were sitting there rest of the day, till it gets dark. Then when it's got dark, we had in mind to which farmer we're going. And we went to that farmer-- it was probably middle of the night already. We knocked on the door, and he came out. And when he saw us, he said, the Lord brought you here. I remembered like this, he said. And we were hungry and cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told us to go in, in his stable. But if we're caught in his stable, we should not tell that he allows-- allowed us to get in, because he was in danger too. And they brought us food, milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were sitting there for-- probably till it started to get real, real cold. So in the daytime, we were in the stable. When the lady went to milk the cows, she had a pail. So she used to have food in it, bring it, leave it, and go and do her job. When it real got cold, they used to take us in their house for the night to sleep-- early in the morning, back on the-- in the-- in the stable. And we were there about five or six months, because it was already winter. One night, there's a bunch of fellows with horses came in on his property. And he had a couple horses. And they wanted to take away his horses. And this was Russian partisans. And the only transportation was horses. To him, the horses-- he'll give his wife. But don't touch the horses. No-- they're taking the horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, look. I'm not a collaborator with the Germans or with the Ukrainian police. I hide Jewish people here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took us out. And he says, this is the Jewish people that they were already months in here. So the partisans saw me and my brother, two young fellows. He got mad at us and says, you're hiding behind our skin. We're fighting, and you're hiding. Lets go with us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we told him, as long as you don't take his horses-- because that's his main thing. So they left the horses. And we went away with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I had a pistol. But that's not enough, a pistol, to fight a war. We needed a machine gun. We needed rifles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the beginning, the partisans, when they attacked a little town or a little village where there were maybe a dozen police, they used to attack them. And we went-- after they were killed, we went to take the weapons away from them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's the way we got into the partisans. And my mother is, I thought, is still at that farmer. Oh, a few months later, we drove right through that village with other partisans. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted to come to that farmer. So I said to the fellows, I know a fellow here, a farmer that he makes home brew. Let's go, and we'll get some home brew from him. And we drove up. And sure enough, that farmer used to make home brew. He gave us to drink and to eat. And my mother was there with my sister. Before I left that farmer, I went after the farmer and I tell him, if you ever give up my mother and my sister to the Germans or to the Ukrainian police, I'll come, burn you out, and kill all your family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's happened after that-- he was a Pole-- that the Ukrainians had Ukrainian partisans, too. And any Pole used to live in a Ukrainian territory, they used to burn them out and kill them, too. So that Pole [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took all his belongings and his wife, and he had one son, and took my mother and my sister, and he went away to Polish villages-- that they had a self-defense call against the Ukrainian. And he kept them till 1944. Then, my hometown was liberated. He brought them to the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the partisan group or the leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- well, I remember the name. That was a Russian officer. Probably he was a party man. And he knew if he gets into prison, they'll kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he escaped to the forest. Not only he, but a lot of Russian soldiers and officers. And he organized a partisan group against the Germans, against the police for they used to do terrible things, even to Ukrainian people-- the ones that they were not collaborators.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the name of that officer was Shitov. And he named the partisan group under his name. It was called Shitowski [RUSSIAN]-- that means Shitov's Group. But when I say a group, there were 5,000, 6,000 people in that group. That we-- if, when we used to drive into a village, or to a little town, we took over the complete thing, just like before the war. We did our job, and we returned back where we supposed to return. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And that was up to 19-- to spring 1944. In 1944 in spring, my hometown was already liberated by the Russians. So naturally, I want to go home-- I want to see who survived, who didn't survive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But with the partisans as priority, if I came home, any job I wanted in the city, I was able to get. So actually, we were maybe a dozen Jewish fellows. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we organized, at that time, cult-- [NON-ENGLISH]. What does it mean? A group that they will hunt the collaborators. And I knew all the collaborators in the city. So we went to look for them to find them. We couldn't find them. They escaped with the Nazis. They left their wives.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they lived in Jewish homes. So we threw them out from the Jewish homes. They went back to their farms. And that was going on till about, probably May. It was early spring. I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother and sister-- where were your mother and sister during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were at home. The-- the Pole brought them to the city. And they were at home. And you know, runaway farmers that they knew once, they started to bring flour and bread and milk and cheese, everything. And they were good people, the one that we knew. For an example, we had a friend, a Ukrainian friend-- father, mother, like husband, wife, and two boys. Soon the-- soon the Germans came, the two boys went right away to the police. Mother and father were fine people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The boys became police. They never touched me. I remember Christmas 1943, or '44-- they came to where we were living in-- for the occupation yet. And they walked in, in the house where we were, with their uniforms, Nazi uniforms. And they said, let's go. And they took us on a sleigh, two horses. And they took us to their mother and father for Christmas. And we were there all night, eating and drinking. In the morning, they brought us back to the so-called ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop now and go to tape three.
